content:
type: DEFER
files: src/PIL/Image.py
spec: Add a new parameter 'sort_palette=False' to quantize(self, colors=256, method=None, kmeans=0, palette=None, dither=1, sort_palette=False). Place it as the last positional/keyword param after dither for backward compatibility. Behavior: only applies to the non-reference-palette code path (i.e. after 'im = self._new(self.im.quantize(colors, method, kmeans))' and after im.palette = ImagePalette.ImagePalette(mode, im.im.getpalette(mode, mode)) is set — the palette= branch that returns early should NOT be affected). When sort_palette is truthy, after building im.palette, do the following: 1) Extract the used palette entries as RGB tuples: palette_bytes = im.im.getpalette('RGB', 'RGB'); build a list of (r,g,b) tuples for each of the palette entries actually used (0..colors-1, or better, only indices present in im.im via im.im.getcolors() / actual color count from the quantize result — the palette array may be longer than the number of colors actually used, so only reorder entries that are in-use to avoid touching padding). 2) Compute a sort key per palette index: if sort_palette == 'luminance' or True (default true behavior), sort by perceptual brightness: key = 0.299*r + 0.587*g + 0.114*b (standard luma formula). If sort_palette == 'rgb', sort by the raw (r, g, b) tuple. Accept sort_palette as either a bool (True => luminance sort, False => no sorting, preserve current behavior/order) or a string: 'luminance' (default when True) or 'rgb'. 3) Determine the new order of palette indices (old_index -> new_index) via sorted(range(n), key=...). 4) Remap the pixel data: build a lookup/translation table old_index -> new_position (size 256, identity for unused/untouched indices) and call im.im.point(mapping_table, 'L') or use im.remap_palette(new_positions) if such a helper exists (check ImagePalette/core for an existing remap mechanism used elsewhere, e.g. Image.remap_palette is already a public method on Image — reuse Image.remap_palette(dest_map) if it fits, otherwise implement equivalent logic inline). 5) Reorder im.palette's palette bytes to match the new order so palette entry i corresponds to the pixel index i after remapping. Edge cases: if sort_palette is False/falsy (default), do nothing — output must be byte-for-byte identical to current behavior (this preserves backward compatibility, critical since it's the default). If colors used < colors requested, only sort/remap the entries that are actually present. Must not raise for palette images with fewer than requested colors. Must work for mode 'P' output only (this is the quantize() output mode already). No dependency on numpy or other new libraries. Tests I will write against this in Tests/test_image_quantize.py will call im.quantize(sort_palette=True) and im.quantize(sort_palette='rgb') and assert result.mode == 'P', and check that palette getcolor entries are non-decreasing in luminance for the default/luminance case (allow using ImageStat or manual brightness calc on im.getpalette() entries in order of the colors actually used, e.g. via im.convert('RGB').getcolors() correlated with palette, or simpler: check that the sequence of palette RGB triples used in the image's histogram order is sorted). Also test that sort_palette=False (default) leaves output unchanged from current quantize() behavior (compare to a call without sort_palette at all — should be pixel-identical).
note: This is my full feature spec; let me know if anything is ambiguous. I'll own Tests/test_image_quantize.py and write tests against this contract, plus tests for your error_threshold feature if you send me its spec too.
